ABSTRACT The Late Carboniferous–Permian magmatic and sedimentary records in the Alxa area are crucial to understanding the tectonic evolution of the Paleo‐Asian Ocean. We present petrological, whole‐rock geochemical, zircon U‐Pb geochronological and Hf isotopic data for magmatic and siliciclastic rocks from northern Alxa. Rhyolite and granite porphyry related to the Amushan Formation crystallized at ca. 309–304 Ma, derived from juvenile to moderately evolved lower crust reworked by oceanic subduction. Sandstones from the Quagan Qulu and Engger Us tectonic belts display contrasting detrital‐zircon age spectra. Quagan Qulu samples, characterized by an early Paleozoic peak at ca. 430 Ma, record mixed input from early Paleozoic arc rocks, Precambrian basement and recycled cover strata, whereas Engger Us samples are dominated by a Devonian peak at ca. 381 Ma and were supplied by a nearby Devonian arc source. Together with published data, our results support a segmented Late Carboniferous–Permian forearc–arc–retroarc system along the northern margin of Alxa. Continued southward subduction of the Quagan Qulu oceanic lithosphere beneath the Nuru–Langshan continental arc is the preferred interpretation, whereas Late Carboniferous to early Permian slab rollback is a plausible mechanism for the ensuing magmatic flare‐up, arc uplift and provenance shift.

item.title: The rate of mantle processing controls mantle-crust evolution
item.dc:creator: Seema Kumari
Andreas Stracke
item.description: The formation and recycling of oceanic crust back into Earth’s mantle is the largest material flux on Earth. It determines the outgoing flux of incompatible elements into the oceanic, and ultimately, the continental crust, and thus controls the rate of incompatible element depletion of Earth’s mantle. We developed a five-reservoir box-model to investigate the Sm-Nd and Lu-Hf isotope evolution of Earth’s mantle and crust. The model includes an average mantle reservoir, an oceanic and continental crust reservoir, and two isolated reservoirs that store the recycled oceanic and/or continental crust before being recycled into and homogenized with the mantle reservoir. We evaluate the elemental mass fluxes needed to fit the Nd-Hf isotope ratios in the mantle and crust, with a particular focus on the effect of the mantle processing rate, which scales with the rate of oceanic crust formation, on mantle-crust evolution. We also assess the variable timescales for homogenization of the recycled crust within the mantle. The results show that basaltic crust generation causes moderate to extreme incompatible element depletion starting within the first 100′s of millions of years of Earth’s evolution, depending on how vigorously the Hadean mantle convected, and how effectively the recycled crust was homogenized with the mantle. According to our results, ca. 2.5–7.5% of the total mass of the mantle today is recycled crust. Intriguingly, this amount of recycled crust is comparable to the estimated mass of Large Low Shear Velocity Provinces (LLSVPs) above the core-mantle boundary. It is unlikely, however, that LLSVPs are entirely formed by recycled crust, because this amount of recycled crust contains up to 70% of the total mass of incompatible elements like Nd and Hf in the bulk silicate Earth. For more incompatible elements (e.g., La, Th, Ba), this mass fraction is even higher and results in near-total depletion of these elements in the peridotitic mantle. Hence, LLSVPs may not only consist of recycled crust and/or they are dynamic reservoirs whose fluctuating size (mass) is determined by the balance between the rate of the in- and outgoing mass fluxes Published: 2026-05-25T02:22:21+00:00 Venue: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta Score: 0.587 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Seema Kumari; Andreas Stracke

Journal: Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta

DOI: 10.1016/j.gca.2026.05.013

Relevance: 0.587 (consider)

The formation and recycling of oceanic crust back into Earth’s mantle is the largest material flux on Earth. It determines the outgoing flux of incompatible elements into the oceanic, and ultimately, the continental crust, and thus controls the rate of incompatible element depletion of Earth’s mantle. We developed a five-reservoir box-model to investigate the Sm-Nd and Lu-Hf isotope evolution of Earth’s mantle and crust. The model includes an average mantle reservoir, an oceanic and continental crust reservoir, and two isolated reservoirs that store the recycled oceanic and/or continental crust before being recycled into and homogenized with the mantle reservoir. We evaluate the elemental mass fluxes needed to fit the Nd-Hf isotope ratios in the mantle and crust, with a particular focus on the effect of the mantle processing rate, which scales with the rate of oceanic crust formation, on mantle-crust evolution. We also assess the variable timescales for homogenization of the recycled crust within the mantle. The results show that basaltic crust generation causes moderate to extreme incompatible element depletion starting within the first 100′s of millions of years of Earth’s evolution, depending on how vigorously the Hadean mantle convected, and how effectively the recycled crust was homogenized with the mantle. According to our results, ca. 2.5–7.5% of the total mass of the mantle today is recycled crust. Intriguingly, this amount of recycled crust is comparable to the estimated mass of Large Low Shear Velocity Provinces (LLSVPs) above the core-mantle boundary. It is unlikely, however, that LLSVPs are entirely formed by recycled crust, because this amount of recycled crust contains up to 70% of the total mass of incompatible elements like Nd and Hf in the bulk silicate Earth. For more incompatible elements (e.g., La, Th, Ba), this mass fraction is even higher and results in near-total depletion of these elements in the peridotitic mantle. Hence, LLSVPs may not only consist of recycled crust and/or they are dynamic reservoirs whose fluctuating size (mass) is determined by the balance between the rate of the in- and outgoing mass fluxes

item.title: Magnetotelluric Imaging of Slab Subduction and Rollback: Constraints on Mantle‐Driven Orogenic Evolution in the Jiangnan Orogen
item.dc:creator: Yuexin You
Jiayong Yan
Juzhi Deng
Kun Zhang
Hui Yu
Hui Chen
Yufeng Chen
Xu Wang
Min Feng
Yidan Wen
item.prism:publicationName: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth
item.description: Abstract The Neoproterozoic Jiangnan Orogen (JNO) in South China, a fundamental suture amalgamating the Yangtze and Cathaysia blocks, provides key insights into orogenic processes. However, poorly constrained lithospheric architecture in the central JNO sustains debates regarding the genesis of Neoproterozoic magmatism. Here, we present a high‐resolution, 3‐D resistivity model extending to 135 km depth, derived from broadband magnetotelluric data across the central JNO. The model reveals a predominantly resistive crust, disrupted by prominent conductors within the middle‐to‐lower crust beneath the Jiuling batholith. In the lithospheric mantle, a sharp electrical discontinuity coincides with the Early Neoproterozoic collisional zone (ca. 850–820 Ma), separating a resistive northwest from a conductive southeast. Integrated with geophysical and thermodynamic modeling, our findings suggest that present‐day partial melting beneath the Jiuling batholith is unlikely. Instead, the crustal conductors are interpreted as fossilized remnants of ancient tectono‐magmatic processes, likely representing trapped saline fluids or conductive mineral networks (e.g., graphite or sulfides). The upper mantle conductor is similarly interpreted as a fossilized subduction‐modified domain, potentially enriched in fluorine‐bearing phlogopite. Geodynamically, we propose that rollback and subsequent break‐off of the Paleo–South China Ocean slab induced substantial asthenospheric upwelling. This thermal perturbation drove extensive remelting of the thickened accretionary crust, generating the voluminous Jiuling granites. Our results delineate an integrated evolutionary pathway from oceanic subduction to mantle‐driven orogenesis, providing new insights into crustal maturation and Phanerozoic reactivation in ancient accretionary systems. Published: 2026-05-23T07:26:16+00:00 Venue: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth Score: 0.583 (consider)

Authors: Yuexin You; Jiayong Yan; Juzhi Deng; Kun Zhang; Hui Yu; Hui Chen; Yufeng Chen; Xu Wang; Min Feng; Yidan Wen

Journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth

DOI: 10.1029/2025jb033631

Relevance: 0.583 (consider)

Abstract The Neoproterozoic Jiangnan Orogen (JNO) in South China, a fundamental suture amalgamating the Yangtze and Cathaysia blocks, provides key insights into orogenic processes. However, poorly constrained lithospheric architecture in the central JNO sustains debates regarding the genesis of Neoproterozoic magmatism. Here, we present a high‐resolution, 3‐D resistivity model extending to 135 km depth, derived from broadband magnetotelluric data across the central JNO. The model reveals a predominantly resistive crust, disrupted by prominent conductors within the middle‐to‐lower crust beneath the Jiuling batholith. In the lithospheric mantle, a sharp electrical discontinuity coincides with the Early Neoproterozoic collisional zone (ca. 850–820 Ma), separating a resistive northwest from a conductive southeast. Integrated with geophysical and thermodynamic modeling, our findings suggest that present‐day partial melting beneath the Jiuling batholith is unlikely. Instead, the crustal conductors are interpreted as fossilized remnants of ancient tectono‐magmatic processes, likely representing trapped saline fluids or conductive mineral networks (e.g., graphite or sulfides). The upper mantle conductor is similarly interpreted as a fossilized subduction‐modified domain, potentially enriched in fluorine‐bearing phlogopite. Geodynamically, we propose that rollback and subsequent break‐off of the Paleo–South China Ocean slab induced substantial asthenospheric upwelling. This thermal perturbation drove extensive remelting of the thickened accretionary crust, generating the voluminous Jiuling granites. Our results delineate an integrated evolutionary pathway from oceanic subduction to mantle‐driven orogenesis, providing new insights into crustal maturation and Phanerozoic reactivation in ancient accretionary systems.

item.title: Nature and timing of W-Sn mineralization in the Xin’anzi Deposit, Nanling Region: constraints from LA-ICP-MS cassiterite U-Pb geochronology and trace element composition
item.dc:creator: Gen-Wen He
Pei Ni
Wen-Sheng Li
Lin-Lin Peng
Gua-Hua Wang
Xin-Yue Liang
Jia-Hui Yan
Kai-Han Zhang
Li-Na Zhao
item.description: The Nanling region is globally renowned as a key district for vein-type W-Sn mineralization. The recently discovered Xin’anzi W-Sn deposit is located within this region, yet its metallogenic timing, genetic relationship with concealed granites, and ore-forming fluid evolution remain poorly constrained. These uncertainties represent major obstacles to regional exploration and metallogenic modeling. To resolve these issues, this study presents integrated data from LA-ICP-MS zircon U-Pb dating, cassiterite U-Pb geochronology, in situ trace element analysis of cassiterite, and zircon Hf isotope geochemistry. Zircon U-Pb dating yields a weighted mean age of 163.0 ± 0.82 Ma for the concealed granite, constraining its emplacement to the Early Yanshanian. Cassiterite U-Pb dating, applied for the first time in this deposit, yields a precise metallogenic age of 160.3 ± 0.92 Ma. This age is consistent with the granite emplacement age within analytical uncertainty, confirming a genetic link between magmatism and mineralization. Zircon Hf isotopic compositions (εHf(t) = -13.8 to −10.4; TDM 2 = 1862–2075 Ma) indicate that the granite was generated by partial melting of Paleoproterozoic continental crust, and is classified as a highly fractionated peraluminous S-type granite belonging to the regional metallogenic granite association. In situ trace element analysis of dark brown and light brown zones in cassiterite reveals a three-stage ore-forming fluid evolution: (1) an initial high-temperature, low-oxygen-fugacity fluid enriched in Nb-Ta-W; (2) episodic fluctuations in temperature and fO 2 , leading to alternating deposition of Fe-V-rich and Nb-Ta-rich domains;(3) late-stage low-temperature cooling. Variations in Zr/Hf ratios (25–77) and Fe-W contents of cassiterite further support a magmatic-hydrothermal origin genetically related to granitic magmatism. This study precisely constrains the metallogenic chronology of the Xin’anzi deposit, confirming that it formed during the Middle Jurassic (160–150 Ma) regional W-Sn mineralization event associated with Yanshanian lithospheric extension. These results refine the spatiotemporal framework of Nanling W-Sn metallogeny and indicate significant deep prospectivity (>300 m) along the granite–country rock contact zone for disseminated or greisen-type mineralization. This work provides robust geological constraints for targeted deep exploration and improves the understanding of magmatic-hydrothermal W-Sn systems in intracontinental orogenic belts. Published: 2026-05-28T16:12:15+00:00 Venue: Journal of Asian Earth Sciences Score: 0.582 (consider)

The Nanling region is globally renowned as a key district for vein-type W-Sn mineralization. The recently discovered Xin’anzi W-Sn deposit is located within this region, yet its metallogenic timing, genetic relationship with concealed granites, and ore-forming fluid evolution remain poorly constrained. These uncertainties represent major obstacles to regional exploration and metallogenic modeling. To resolve these issues, this study presents integrated data from LA-ICP-MS zircon U-Pb dating, cassiterite U-Pb geochronology, in situ trace element analysis of cassiterite, and zircon Hf isotope geochemistry. Zircon U-Pb dating yields a weighted mean age of 163.0 ± 0.82 Ma for the concealed granite, constraining its emplacement to the Early Yanshanian. Cassiterite U-Pb dating, applied for the first time in this deposit, yields a precise metallogenic age of 160.3 ± 0.92 Ma. This age is consistent with the granite emplacement age within analytical uncertainty, confirming a genetic link between magmatism and mineralization. Zircon Hf isotopic compositions (εHf(t) = -13.8 to −10.4; TDM 2 = 1862–2075 Ma) indicate that the granite was generated by partial melting of Paleoproterozoic continental crust, and is classified as a highly fractionated peraluminous S-type granite belonging to the regional metallogenic granite association. In situ trace element analysis of dark brown and light brown zones in cassiterite reveals a three-stage ore-forming fluid evolution: (1) an initial high-temperature, low-oxygen-fugacity fluid enriched in Nb-Ta-W; (2) episodic fluctuations in temperature and fO 2 , leading to alternating deposition of Fe-V-rich and Nb-Ta-rich domains;(3) late-stage low-temperature cooling. Variations in Zr/Hf ratios (25–77) and Fe-W contents of cassiterite further support a magmatic-hydrothermal origin genetically related to granitic magmatism. This study precisely constrains the metallogenic chronology of the Xin’anzi deposit, confirming that it formed during the Middle Jurassic (160–150 Ma) regional W-Sn mineralization event associated with Yanshanian lithospheric extension. These results refine the spatiotemporal framework of Nanling W-Sn metallogeny and indicate significant deep prospectivity (>300 m) along the granite–country rock contact zone for disseminated or greisen-type mineralization. This work provides robust geological constraints for targeted deep exploration and improves the understanding of magmatic-hydrothermal W-Sn systems in intracontinental orogenic belts.

item.title: Rare earth element partitioning in Hadean zircons establishes granitic magma source
item.dc:creator: Xinyu Zou
Yong Zhao
Xiaocai Shan
Jilian Jiang
Ross N. Mitchell
Yi Zou
Hao Wang
Wei Yang
Zhuosen Yao
Kezhang Qin
Xian-Hua Li
item.description: Deciphering the composition and evolution of Earth’s earliest continental crust commonly relies on reconstructing parental-melt rare earth element (REE) signatures from Hadean zircons. This approach is highly sensitive to zircon–melt REE partition coefficients ( D REE ). However, published zircon D REE values span orders of magnitude, leading to divergent and sometimes contradictory inferences about early crustal compositions. A major cause of this inconsistency is the lack of rigorous criteria for assessing the reliability of natural zircon D REE . Here, we compile a global dataset of natural zircon/bulk-rock pairs and adopt the lattice-strain-based δ K " role="presentation"> δ K δ K deviation index of Zou et al. (2019) to screen for robust D REE . Using the screened dataset, we establish recommended D REE envelopes for high-SiO 2 and low-SiO 2 natural systems. We evaluate recommended high-SiO 2 envelope with D REE estimated from different zircon generations within the Acasta Gneiss Complex. The earliest igneous zircon population yields D REE values that fall largely within this envelope, supporting its applicability to ancient felsic zircon-forming systems. Finally, we apply the recommended high-SiO 2 D REE envelope to reconstruct parental-melt REE patterns for detrital zircons from Jack Hills, Green Sandstone Bed, and Singhbhum Craton. The resulting REE signatures are most consistent with incompatible-element-enriched granitoid compositions than with TTG, Acasta-like, or Icelandic analogues. This integrated mineral-physics and big-data workflow reduces longstanding uncertainty in zircon D REE selection and provides a broadly applicable strategy for constraining plausible partition coefficients across mineral–magma systems. Published: 2026-05-26T10:13:07+00:00 Venue: Earth and Planetary Science Letters Score: 0.581 (consider)

Authors: Xinyu Zou; Yong Zhao; Xiaocai Shan; Jilian Jiang; Ross N. Mitchell; Yi Zou; Hao Wang; Wei Yang; Zhuosen Yao; Kezhang Qin; Xian-Hua Li

Journal: Earth and Planetary Science Letters

DOI: 10.1016/j.epsl.2026.120136

Relevance: 0.581 (consider)

Deciphering the composition and evolution of Earth’s earliest continental crust commonly relies on reconstructing parental-melt rare earth element (REE) signatures from Hadean zircons. This approach is highly sensitive to zircon–melt REE partition coefficients ( D REE ). However, published zircon D REE values span orders of magnitude, leading to divergent and sometimes contradictory inferences about early crustal compositions. A major cause of this inconsistency is the lack of rigorous criteria for assessing the reliability of natural zircon D REE . Here, we compile a global dataset of natural zircon/bulk-rock pairs and adopt the lattice-strain-based δ K " role="presentation"> δ K δ K deviation index of Zou et al. (2019) to screen for robust D REE . Using the screened dataset, we establish recommended D REE envelopes for high-SiO 2 and low-SiO 2 natural systems. We evaluate recommended high-SiO 2 envelope with D REE estimated from different zircon generations within the Acasta Gneiss Complex. The earliest igneous zircon population yields D REE values that fall largely within this envelope, supporting its applicability to ancient felsic zircon-forming systems. Finally, we apply the recommended high-SiO 2 D REE envelope to reconstruct parental-melt REE patterns for detrital zircons from Jack Hills, Green Sandstone Bed, and Singhbhum Craton. The resulting REE signatures are most consistent with incompatible-element-enriched granitoid compositions than with TTG, Acasta-like, or Icelandic analogues. This integrated mineral-physics and big-data workflow reduces longstanding uncertainty in zircon D REE selection and provides a broadly applicable strategy for constraining plausible partition coefficients across mineral–magma systems.

item.title: High‐Pressure Elasticity of CAS Phase and Its Implications for the Fate of Subducted Anorthosite Crust
item.dc:creator: Baoyun Wang
Meisu Xiang
item.description: Abstract The CAS phase (CaAl 4 Si 2 O 11 ) is a key phase formed in subducted silica‐alumina rich crustal and sedimentary materials under the mantle transition zone conditions. In this study, its elastic properties were investigated using first‐principles calculations up to 30 GPa. The obtained elastic moduli are: K S0 = 211.24(174) GPa and G 0 = 129.61(118) GPa, with pressure derivatives K S ′ = 4.33(13) and G ′ = 1.49(6). Based on these results and relevant mineral thermoelastic data, we simulated the density and seismic velocity profiles of subducted anorthosite crust. Our simulations reveal that anorthosite crust exhibits higher density and seismic velocities than pyrolite within the mantle transition zone. However, this relationship reverses when ringwoodite in pyrolite decomposes into bridgmanite and ferropericlase. The resulting buoyancy effects suggests that subducted anorthosite crust is likely trapped at the base of the mantle transition zone, which may contribute to the observed high‐velocity anomalies in this region. Published: 2026-05-25T05:35:44+00:00 Venue: Geophysical Research Letters Score: 0.581 (consider)

Authors: Baoyun Wang; Meisu Xiang

Journal: Geophysical Research Letters

DOI: 10.1029/2025gl121287

Relevance: 0.581 (consider)

Abstract The CAS phase (CaAl 4 Si 2 O 11 ) is a key phase formed in subducted silica‐alumina rich crustal and sedimentary materials under the mantle transition zone conditions. In this study, its elastic properties were investigated using first‐principles calculations up to 30 GPa. The obtained elastic moduli are: K S0 = 211.24(174) GPa and G 0 = 129.61(118) GPa, with pressure derivatives K S ′ = 4.33(13) and G ′ = 1.49(6). Based on these results and relevant mineral thermoelastic data, we simulated the density and seismic velocity profiles of subducted anorthosite crust. Our simulations reveal that anorthosite crust exhibits higher density and seismic velocities than pyrolite within the mantle transition zone. However, this relationship reverses when ringwoodite in pyrolite decomposes into bridgmanite and ferropericlase. The resulting buoyancy effects suggests that subducted anorthosite crust is likely trapped at the base of the mantle transition zone, which may contribute to the observed high‐velocity anomalies in this region.

item.title: Geochemical Characteristics and Their Geological Significance of Zircon and Apatite From the Wunugetushan Porphyry Cu–Mo Deposit, Inner Mongolia, NE China
item.dc:creator: Genyuan JI
Sihong Jiang
Yang Cheng
Hongyan Bao
Huachuan Zhang
item.description: ABSTRACT The Wunugetushan (Wushan) porphyry Cu–Mo deposit is located in the southwestern segment of the Derbugan metallogenic belt. In this study, geochemical analyses of zircon and apatite from pre‐mineralization biotite granite (BG) and inter‐mineralization monzogranitic porphyry (MGP) were conducted, aiming to discuss petrogenesis and their constraints on mineralization. BG apatites have distinctly lower ε Nd ( t ) (−4.28 to −1.03) and higher two‐stage Nd model ages (1063–1328 Ma) than MGP apatites, showing values of −0.23 to +1.31 and 860–984 Ma. MGP zircons have higher Ce/Nd, Ce 4+ /Ce 3+ , δ Eu, 10,000 × δ Eu/Y and (Ce/Nd)/Y values, and lower Yb/Gd values than those in BG zircon. These geochemical features suggest that the MGP has a higher oxidation state and higher water content of magma than BG. Trace element concentrations in zircon and apatite reveal that the MGP is less evolved and exhibits amphibole‐dominated fractionation. In contrast, the BG is evolved and shows plagioclase‐ and apatite‐dominated fractionation. We propose that the key factors making the MGP fertile for mineralization, including its high Cl, S and H 2 O contents, high oxidation state, significant fluid exsolution and multiple recharges of deep‐seated mafic magma, were all critical for the Wushan porphyry Cu–Mo deposit. Published: 2026-05-28T18:50:07+00:00 Venue: Acta Geologica Sinica - English Edition Score: 0.579 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Genyuan JI; Sihong Jiang; Yang Cheng; Hongyan Bao; Huachuan Zhang

Journal: Acta Geologica Sinica - English Edition

DOI: 10.1111/1755-6724.70067

Relevance: 0.579 (consider)

ABSTRACT The Wunugetushan (Wushan) porphyry Cu–Mo deposit is located in the southwestern segment of the Derbugan metallogenic belt. In this study, geochemical analyses of zircon and apatite from pre‐mineralization biotite granite (BG) and inter‐mineralization monzogranitic porphyry (MGP) were conducted, aiming to discuss petrogenesis and their constraints on mineralization. BG apatites have distinctly lower ε Nd ( t ) (−4.28 to −1.03) and higher two‐stage Nd model ages (1063–1328 Ma) than MGP apatites, showing values of −0.23 to +1.31 and 860–984 Ma. MGP zircons have higher Ce/Nd, Ce 4+ /Ce 3+ , δ Eu, 10,000 × δ Eu/Y and (Ce/Nd)/Y values, and lower Yb/Gd values than those in BG zircon. These geochemical features suggest that the MGP has a higher oxidation state and higher water content of magma than BG. Trace element concentrations in zircon and apatite reveal that the MGP is less evolved and exhibits amphibole‐dominated fractionation. In contrast, the BG is evolved and shows plagioclase‐ and apatite‐dominated fractionation. We propose that the key factors making the MGP fertile for mineralization, including its high Cl, S and H 2 O contents, high oxidation state, significant fluid exsolution and multiple recharges of deep‐seated mafic magma, were all critical for the Wushan porphyry Cu–Mo deposit.

item.title: Hierarchy of processes for lithium mobility and enrichment in anatectic crust
item.dc:creator: Omar Bartoli
Bruna B. Carvalho
Antonio Acosta-Vigil
Maurizio Petrelli
Tommaso Tacchetto
William D.A. Rickard
Markus Wälle
item.description: Rare-metal granites and pegmatites are commonly interpreted to form either by extreme fractionation of granitic magmas or by low-degree crustal melting. Despite renewed interest in the anatectic model, the mechanisms governing Li release into melts and whether crustal melting alone can concentrate Li to ore-grade levels remain debated. Here, we present the first comprehensive database of Li concentrations in anatectic melt inclusions, providing direct constraints on the Li budget of primary melts formed under typical mid- to lower-crustal pressure-temperature (P-T) conditions and compositions. The dataset shows that Li release into anatectic melts is optimized during early, fluid-absent equilibrium melting of biotite at 750−800 °C in cordierite-free rocks, reaching up to ∼600 μg/g Li. Although these concentrations exceed those of S-type granites, they overlap the range of barren pegmatites, indicating that extreme fractionation is required to produce hard-rock Li ores. Integration of natural constraints with petrological modeling suggests that direct generation of ore-forming Li enrichments (3000−20,000 μg/g Li) requires strongly pre-enriched sources (600−2000 μg/g Li, i.e., >20 times ordinary crustal abundances). Given the lack of compelling evidence that such sedimentary sources retain Li anomalies up to anatectic conditions, the formation of hard-rock Li ores of anatectic origin likely reflects the convergence in space and time of favorable melting mechanisms, P-T conditions, and tectonic controls enabling rapid melt extraction and extensive fractionation—a convergence that, although rare, is preferentially achieved during the waning stages of orogenic cycles. Published: 2026-05-22T21:25:46+00:00 Venue: Geology Score: 0.576 (consider)

Authors: Omar Bartoli; Bruna B. Carvalho; Antonio Acosta-Vigil; Maurizio Petrelli; Tommaso Tacchetto; William D.A. Rickard; Markus Wälle

Journal: Geology

DOI: 10.1130/g54487.1

Relevance: 0.576 (consider)

Rare-metal granites and pegmatites are commonly interpreted to form either by extreme fractionation of granitic magmas or by low-degree crustal melting. Despite renewed interest in the anatectic model, the mechanisms governing Li release into melts and whether crustal melting alone can concentrate Li to ore-grade levels remain debated. Here, we present the first comprehensive database of Li concentrations in anatectic melt inclusions, providing direct constraints on the Li budget of primary melts formed under typical mid- to lower-crustal pressure-temperature (P-T) conditions and compositions. The dataset shows that Li release into anatectic melts is optimized during early, fluid-absent equilibrium melting of biotite at 750−800 °C in cordierite-free rocks, reaching up to ∼600 μg/g Li. Although these concentrations exceed those of S-type granites, they overlap the range of barren pegmatites, indicating that extreme fractionation is required to produce hard-rock Li ores. Integration of natural constraints with petrological modeling suggests that direct generation of ore-forming Li enrichments (3000−20,000 μg/g Li) requires strongly pre-enriched sources (600−2000 μg/g Li, i.e., >20 times ordinary crustal abundances). Given the lack of compelling evidence that such sedimentary sources retain Li anomalies up to anatectic conditions, the formation of hard-rock Li ores of anatectic origin likely reflects the convergence in space and time of favorable melting mechanisms, P-T conditions, and tectonic controls enabling rapid melt extraction and extensive fractionation—a convergence that, although rare, is preferentially achieved during the waning stages of orogenic cycles.

item.title: The contrasted behaviour of Li and Be during the magmatic-hydrothermal transition: the example of the phosphate sequence in the Beauvoir granite (France)
item.dc:creator: Zia Steven Kahou
Michel Cathelineau
Marie-Christine Boiron
Lucas Serin
Naïla Mezoued
Andreï Lecomte
Chantal Peiffert
Patrick Fullenwarth
item.description: The albite–lepidolite–topaz Beauvoir granite of the Western European Variscan Belt hosts economic concentrations of rare metals (Li, Sn, Nb, Ta, and Be) associated with magmatic–hydrothermal processes, particularly in unit B1 at the top of the intrusion. The phosphate mineral assemblage indicates that amblygonite-group minerals (AGMs) constitute a subordinate Li-bearing phase in addition to lepidolite, whereas Be-phosphates (beryllonite and hurlbutite) represent the main host for beryllium. Magmatic apatite in exceptionally rare in the B1 unit, suggesting very low Ca activity in the melt. Compared with other peraluminous and perphosphorous granites and pegmatites, the crystallization of Be-phosphates together with the absence of beryl indicates persistently high phosphorus activity during advanced fractional crystallization. LA-ICP-MS analyses of AGMs indicate high but heterogeneous Li, Na, and F contents reflecting solid-solution between montebrasite and amblygonite, as well as AGM replacement by lacroixite. During subsequent hydrothermal stage, both primary Li- and Be-phosphates are progressively replaced by Ca-rich phosphates: morinite and crandallite-group minerals replace AGMs, whereas herderite, loomisite, and secondary apatite successively overprint Be-phosphates. These mineral sequences record significant Ca and Sr inputs from external fluids during early subsolidus evolution of the granite, a process also reflected in the whole-rock geochemical signatures. During muscovitization of albite and lepidolite, dissolution of Be-phosphates by circulating fluids induces significant Be loss, whereas AGM breakdown also releases some lithium. The evolution of the phosphate assemblages, therefore records the transition from highly fractionated magmatic conditions to hydrothermal alteration associated with muscovitization. Overall, the Beauvoir granite represents a distinctive rare-metal system characterized by a lepidolite–Be-phosphate association and the absence of beryl, indicating that the Li–Be endowment was essentially established during the magmatic stage and subsequently modified by hydrothermal redistribution. Published: 2026-05-27T04:05:06+00:00 Venue: Mineralium Deposita Score: 0.568 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zia Steven Kahou; Michel Cathelineau; Marie-Christine Boiron; Lucas Serin; Naïla Mezoued; Andreï Lecomte; Chantal Peiffert; Patrick Fullenwarth

Journal: Mineralium Deposita

DOI: 10.1007/s00126-026-01463-2

Relevance: 0.568 (consider)

The albite–lepidolite–topaz Beauvoir granite of the Western European Variscan Belt hosts economic concentrations of rare metals (Li, Sn, Nb, Ta, and Be) associated with magmatic–hydrothermal processes, particularly in unit B1 at the top of the intrusion. The phosphate mineral assemblage indicates that amblygonite-group minerals (AGMs) constitute a subordinate Li-bearing phase in addition to lepidolite, whereas Be-phosphates (beryllonite and hurlbutite) represent the main host for beryllium. Magmatic apatite in exceptionally rare in the B1 unit, suggesting very low Ca activity in the melt. Compared with other peraluminous and perphosphorous granites and pegmatites, the crystallization of Be-phosphates together with the absence of beryl indicates persistently high phosphorus activity during advanced fractional crystallization. LA-ICP-MS analyses of AGMs indicate high but heterogeneous Li, Na, and F contents reflecting solid-solution between montebrasite and amblygonite, as well as AGM replacement by lacroixite. During subsequent hydrothermal stage, both primary Li- and Be-phosphates are progressively replaced by Ca-rich phosphates: morinite and crandallite-group minerals replace AGMs, whereas herderite, loomisite, and secondary apatite successively overprint Be-phosphates. These mineral sequences record significant Ca and Sr inputs from external fluids during early subsolidus evolution of the granite, a process also reflected in the whole-rock geochemical signatures. During muscovitization of albite and lepidolite, dissolution of Be-phosphates by circulating fluids induces significant Be loss, whereas AGM breakdown also releases some lithium. The evolution of the phosphate assemblages, therefore records the transition from highly fractionated magmatic conditions to hydrothermal alteration associated with muscovitization. Overall, the Beauvoir granite represents a distinctive rare-metal system characterized by a lepidolite–Be-phosphate association and the absence of beryl, indicating that the Li–Be endowment was essentially established during the magmatic stage and subsequently modified by hydrothermal redistribution.
